food_name: Kung Pao Chicken (Gong Bao Style Stir-Fried Chicken)
aliases: Stir-fried chicken with dried chillies and peanuts

english: Stir-fried chicken with dried chillies and peanuts

region_of_origin: Sichuan, China; widely adopted in Singapore Zi Char cuisine

ontology.foodon_gap_note: No FoodOn term exists specifically for Kung Pao / Gong Bao chicken. FOODON_00002817 (chicken dish) is the closest defensible parent class.
ontology.foodex2_gap_note: FoodEx2 does not have a specific code for Kung Pao / Gong Bao chicken stir-fry. A0CKP (cooked chicken meat) is used as the closest defensible parent. The dish includes additional ingredients (peanuts, dried chillies, sauce) not captured by this code.
ontology.snomed_ct_gap_note: No SNOMED CT concept found for Kung Pao / Gong Bao chicken as a prepared dish. SNOMED CT food concepts at this specificity are not available.

serving.natural_language_portion: A typical restaurant serving of Gong Bao style stir-fried chicken is approximately 300 g, comprising diced chicken pieces, peanuts, dried chillies, and sauce, served as a shared dish alongside steamed rice.

nutrient_source.title: Kung Pao Chicken (Gong Bao Ji Ding) — nutrient profile estimated from USDA FoodData Central SR Legacy entries for cooked chicken breast, roasted peanuts, and standard Kung Pao sauce components, cross-referenced with HPB Singapore nutrient data for stir-fried chicken dishes
nutrient_source.organization: USDA Agricultural Research Service (FoodData Central SR Legacy); Health Promotion Board Singapore (HPB Foodo)
nutrient_source.source_match_notes: No single approved-source entry exists for Gong Bao / Kung Pao chicken as a complete dish. Values are estimated by weighted composition: approximately 55% cooked chicken (USDA SR Legacy NDB 05064 — chicken breast, roasted), 10% roasted peanuts (USDA SR Legacy NDB 16087), 5% cooking oil, and 30% sauce/vegetables/chillies. HPB Singapore stir-fried chicken dishes were used as a plausibility cross-check. Energy calculated from macros using Atwater factors.

gi_source.title: International tables of glycemic index and glycemic load values: 2021 — mixed protein-fat-carbohydrate stir-fried chicken dish analogue; Atkinson FS et al.
gi_source.organization: University of Sydney GI Database; American Journal of Clinical Nutrition
gi_source.source_match_notes: No direct GI measurement exists for Gong Bao / Kung Pao chicken. GI estimated at 35 based on analogous mixed protein-fat-carbohydrate stir-fried chicken dishes from the University of Sydney GI Database and Atkinson et al. (2021) international GI tables. The dish is predominantly protein and fat with modest carbohydrate from sauce and peanuts; the fat and protein content substantially attenuates glycemic response. GL per serving calculated as (GI × carbohydrate_g per serving) / 100 = (35 × 19.5) / 100 ≈ 6.8, where carbohydrate per 300 g serving = 6.5 × 3 = 19.5 g.

hypertension.key_factor: High sodium content from soy sauce and seasoning
hypertension.guidance: Gong Bao chicken contains approximately 520 mg sodium per 100 g (approximately 1560 mg per restaurant serving), which represents a substantial proportion of the recommended daily sodium limit of 2000 mg for hypertensive individuals; patients should request reduced-soy-sauce preparation or limit portion size to half a serving.

type2_diabetes.key_factor: Low glycemic index and moderate carbohydrate content
type2_diabetes.guidance: With a GI of approximately 35 and a glycemic load of approximately 6.8 per restaurant serving, Gong Bao chicken has a low glycemic impact and is generally suitable for individuals with Type 2 Diabetes when consumed without large quantities of steamed rice; the protein and fat content further blunts postprandial glucose response.

cardiovascular_disease.key_factor: Elevated sodium and moderate fat from cooking oil and peanuts
cardiovascular_disease.guidance: The high sodium content (approximately 1560 mg per serving) poses a cardiovascular risk for individuals with existing heart disease or hypertension; however, the unsaturated fat contribution from peanuts and the lean protein from chicken are relatively favourable, and individuals with cardiovascular disease should limit portion size and request low-sodium preparation.

llm_training.natural_language_description: Gong Bao (Kung Pao) style stir-fried chicken is a classic Chinese dish originating from Sichuan province, widely adopted and served in Singaporean Zi Char restaurants and Chinese-Malaysian eateries. The dish consists of diced chicken (typically breast or thigh), stir-fried at high heat in a wok with dried red chillies, roasted peanuts, Sichuan peppercorns, garlic, ginger, and spring onions, bound together with a savoury-sweet-sour sauce made from soy sauce, Shaoxing rice wine, sugar, vinegar, and cornstarch. The result is a dish with bold, complex flavours — simultaneously spicy, numbing (from Sichuan peppercorns), sweet, sour, and umami-rich — with tender chicken pieces contrasting with the crunch of peanuts. In Singapore Zi Char contexts, the dish is typically less numbing than its Sichuan original and may be adjusted for local palates with reduced Sichuan peppercorn and increased sweetness. Nutritionally, Gong Bao chicken is a moderate-calorie, high-protein dish (approximately 175 kcal and 14.8 g protein per 100 g) with moderate fat from cooking oil and peanuts. Sodium content is high due to soy sauce, making it a concern for hypertensive individuals. The glycemic index is low (approximately 35), making it suitable for individuals managing Type 2 Diabetes when consumed without excessive rice. The dish is a good source of niacin, vitamin B6, phosphorus, and selenium from the chicken component, and provides heart-healthy unsaturated fats from peanuts. It is not suitable for individuals with peanut, soy, or sesame allergies.

Is Gong Bao chicken suitable for diabetics?

confidence_reason: No single approved-source entry exists for Gong Bao / Kung Pao chicken as a complete prepared dish in HPB Foodo, ICMR-NIN, or IMR Malaysia MyFCD. Nutrient values are estimated by weighted composition from USDA SR Legacy entries for the primary components (cooked chicken, roasted peanuts, cooking oil) and cross-checked against HPB Singapore stir-fried chicken dish profiles. GI is an analogous estimate from the University of Sydney GI Database. Confidence is therefore medium rather than high.
notes: 1. Nutrient values are compositional estimates; no exact match was found in HPB Foodo, ICMR-NIN, or IMR Malaysia MyFCD for this specific dish. USDA SR Legacy was used as the primary nutrient anchor with HPB plausibility cross-check. 2. Vitamin D is -1 as it is not reported in the USDA SR Legacy entries for cooked chicken or peanuts at the level of detail available for this composite estimate. 3. Trans fat is reported as 0.0 g; this is confirmed zero per USDA SR Legacy data for the primary ingredients (chicken breast, peanuts, vegetable oil) — no industrial trans fats are present in the standard recipe. 4. GI of 35 is an analogous estimate based on mixed protein-fat-carbohydrate stir-fried chicken dishes from the University of Sydney GI Database (Atkinson et al., 2021); no direct GI measurement for Gong Bao chicken was found. 5. Halal status is marked false as the dish may contain Shaoxing rice wine (alcohol); halal-certified versions exist but cannot be assumed for all restaurants. 6. The input record listed cuisine as 'Malaysian' and cuisine tags included 'Zi Char'; the dish is classified here as Chinese (Singaporean Zi Char) as Gong Bao chicken is a Chinese-origin dish served in Zi Char and Malaysian Chinese contexts. 7. Monounsaturated_fat_g, polyunsaturated_fat_g, potassium_mg, calcium_mg, iron_mg, cholesterol_mg, vitamin_c_mg, vitamin_a_mcg_rae, and all B-vitamins are estimated from USDA SR Legacy component data and should be treated as approximate. 8. The field dataed in the input (Marina Seafood House — Boat Quay) is a Zi Char restaurant; the dish is a standard Zi Char menu item.
